questions.prompt: "Young people are all digital natives — they naturally know how to use technology." What does the evidence say?
choices: True — growing up with phones teaches everything
True, but only for people under 20
Mostly a myth — using apps fluently is not the same as judging information, managing privacy, or fixing problems
It is impossible to study
questions.explanation: Familiarity is not literacy. Many fluent scrollers have never been shown how to check a source, recover an account, or spot a scam — those are taught skills, at any age.

questions.prompt: "People who are offline just do not want the internet." What actually keeps most people offline?
choices: Cost, coverage, devices, and skills — barriers more than choices
Lack of interest, almost always
Free public Wi-Fi is everywhere now, so cost is no longer the problem
Nothing — everyone is online now
questions.explanation: Surveys across countries keep finding the same wall: connection and device costs, weak coverage, and never having been taught. Framing it as a choice hides a solvable problem.

questions.prompt: "Having a smartphone means someone is fully online." What does phone-only access make hard?
choices: Nothing — a phone does everything a computer does
Only gaming
Phones cannot open websites
Long forms, homework, job applications, and anything needing a big screen or a keyboard
questions.explanation: A phone on prepaid data is a narrow doorway: fine for messages, hard for a job application, a school essay, or a government form. That gap is called smartphone-dependent access.

questions.prompt: "Accessibility features only matter to a small group of people." Who actually uses them?
choices: Only people with permanent disabilities
Almost everyone at some point — captions on a bus, bigger text with aging eyes, voice input with full hands
Only developers testing their apps
Nobody — they are rarely switched on
questions.explanation: Ability changes with situation and age. Features cut for disability — like captions and voice control — end up serving everyone, which is why removing them harms everyone too.

questions.prompt: A community wants more people online. What tends to help most?
choices: Handing out devices and considering it done
Waiting for the next generation to grow up
Affordable connections plus patient, local, hands-on teaching — libraries and community centers do this well
Making the internet a requirement for all services immediately
questions.explanation: Devices without teaching gather dust, and requirements without access lock people out. Programs that pair affordable connections with a trusted person to learn from are the ones that stick.
